text: The surgeon inspects the small bowel and identifies a large Meckel's diverticulum located approximately two feet from the ileocecal valve, in its typical anatomical position.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic GIE stapler to transect the identified Meckel's diverticulum, which is located approximately two feet from the ileocecal valve.
The surgeon assesses the anterior lumen of the small bowel to ensure adequate space, preventing potential small bowel obstruction during the procedure.
The surgeon evaluates the anterior and posterior aspects of the small bowel resection site, noting a potential narrowing of the small bowel lumen posteriorly.
The surgeon uses a power GIE stapler to perform the transection, carefully checking the site before firing the stapler to ensure proper alignment and closure.
The surgeon fires the GIA stapler across the small bowel lumen, ensuring it is adequate to prevent obstruction, completing the transection without complication.
The surgeon inspects the staple line on the small bowel, confirming that it is completely intact with no visible areas of concern.
The surgeon introduces an endocatch bag into the surgical field, places the resected specimen inside, and successfully removes it from the operative area without complications.
